rule: Counterpoint must be in the same key as the cantus firmus; borrowing a note outside the key incurs a cost.
No chromatic melodies — three consecutive notes ascending by a semitone are prohibited.
Prefer smaller melodic intervals; tritones are last-resort.
The last chord must be in the scale/fundamental of the composition.
The harmonic intervals on the first note of each measure (downbeat/thesis) must be consonances.
After a dissonance, the rules around five consecutive notes by stepwise (joint) degree govern where dissonance is allowed.
The first harmonic interval must be a perfect consonance (2-voice compositions).
Arsis (weak-beat) harmonies cannot be dissonant.
A dissonance implies diminution — it must be written as a passing or neighbor figure between two consonances.
The last chord must be a perfect consonance (2-voice).
The penultimate note has a restricted domain (must support the 2-1/7-1 approach to the final).
Cambiata figure is soft-preferred as a dissonance-handling idiom.
CP and CF must not play the same note, except at the first and last measure.
Inter-voice: two upper counterpoint voices must not play the same note (except first and last).
Imperfect consonances are preferred; perfect 5ths and octaves carry a soft cost.
The penultimate note must form a major 6th (when CP is upper) or a minor 3rd (when CP is lower) with the CF.
Penultimate-interval rule, 3-voice version: depends on stratum position.
Prefer the complete harmonic triad as often as possible (3 voices).
Prefer the complete harmonic triad as often as possible (4 voices).
Melodic intervals cannot exceed a minor 6th (2-voice).
Melodic intervals cannot exceed a minor 6th; an octave leap is permitted (3-voice).
If two voices are too close for contrary motion without crossing, a melodic octave leap becomes permissible.
Variety: notes in each part should be as diverse as possible (discourages repetition within a part).
Two consecutive notes in the same voice should not be the same pitch (2-voice).
Two consecutive notes in the same voice should not be the same pitch (3-voice).
No direct (similar) motion into a perfect consonance (2v, 1st-species variant).
No direct motion into a perfect consonance (3v, 1st-species variant).
No direct motion into a perfect consonance (4v, 1st-species variant).
No direct motion into a perfect consonance (2v, 2nd-species variant).
No direct motion into a perfect consonance (3v, 2nd-species variant).
No direct motion into a perfect consonance (4v, 2nd-species variant).
No 'battuta' — forbid the struck stepwise descent to an octave on a strong beat (default variant).
No battuta — 1st-species variant.
No battuta — 2nd-species variant.
Successive direct (similar) motion incurs a cost.
All voices should not move in the same direction simultaneously (3v).
All voices should not move in the same direction simultaneously (4v).
No successive ascending sixth leaps.
When two voices are in 5th species simultaneously (3v), their rhythmic patterns should be as diverse as possible.
